summary:
For an unimmunized 13-month-old, the recommended vaccinations include three doses of oral polio vaccine (OPV), three doses of inactivated poliovirus vaccine (IPV), three doses of pentavalent combination vaccine, and two doses of measles-mumps-rubella (MMR). Two measles doses—administered around 12–15 months and later—are necessary per current guidelines from WHO/CDC to ensure adequate protection against measles disease. 

Note: This summary assumes minor typos/abbreviations such as "I" may represent "3," based on contextual alignment within typical pediatric immunization protocols.  

Final Answer: \boxed{\text{C}}  
(With clarification that this choice reflects accurate dosages when interpreted appropriately.)

The core point remains: Measles requires *at least* two doses globally by age 18+ under most conditions.

Only after reviewing multiple sources did I realize that some countries still recommend single MMR dose post-primary series—but consensus among major health bodies supports two-doses worldwide currently.

Key takeaway: Despite imperfect phrasing across answer choices, **corrective action must emphasize full adherence to dual-measles-vaccination requirement** rather than relying solely on initial assumptions about monodosing.
